How to Use This Guide · 02

How to Use This Guide.

A six-step process, from finding the right prompt to a reviewed, ready-to-use output

Every prompt in this guide follows the same simple path. The six steps fall into two stages: first you find and prepare the right prompt, then you generate the output, refine it, and apply your own professional judgement before you use it.

Think of each prompt as a starting point, not a finished solution. The strongest outputs come from combining AI capability with your professional judgement.

Stage 1Find and prepare your prompt
1
Identify your ADDIE phase

Find where you are in the design process, Analyse, Design, Develop, Implement or Evaluate.

→
2
Select a prompt

Choose the prompt from that phase that fits your current task.

→
3
Personalise it for your project

Add your own context before running it.

↓
Stage 2Generate, refine and apply judgement
4
Generate a draft

Run the prompt. The output is a starting point.

→
5
Refine the output

Improve the draft until it is fit for purpose.

→
6
Apply professional judgement

Review every output before using it. Your expertise matters.

Next: See the challenges AI can help solve →

The next section maps the common challenges practitioners face at every ADDIE phase, and shows where AI makes the biggest difference.

AI-Enabled ADDIE Workflow · 03

AI-Enabled ADDIE Workflow.

How great prompts transform every phase of learning design

01
Analyse
From assumptions to clear diagnosis
Common Challenges
  • Stakeholder interviews are time-intensive to schedule and complete
  • Training requests often arrive before a full needs analysis is feasible
AI-Assisted Opportunities
  • Synthesise interview notes in minutes
  • Challenge the training request with evidence
02
Design
From ideas to structured solutions
Common Challenges
  • Writing and validating objectives is a time-intensive iterative process
  • Moving from brief to blueprint typically spans multiple working days
AI-Assisted Opportunities
  • Bloom's-aligned objectives in minutes
  • Blueprint drafted in hours, not days
03
Develop
From concept to real learning assets
Common Challenges
  • Every new project starts content development from scratch
  • Review cycles extend timelines and consume significant capacity
AI-Assisted Opportunities
  • First draft generated from your brief
  • Closer to finished on the first pass
04
Implement
From content to adoption
Common Challenges
  • Launch communications compete for time in tight project schedules
  • Learner uptake is shaped by contextual factors beyond the design itself
AI-Assisted Opportunities
  • Comms strategy built into the design
  • Messaging tailored to audience segments
05
Evaluate
From activity to measurable impact
Common Challenges
  • Evaluation planning is regularly deprioritised under time and budget pressure
  • Isolating and evidencing learning impact is methodologically complex
AI-Assisted Opportunities
  • Survey design fast enough to actually do
  • Impact story built from evidence
Key ShiftGreat prompts do more than save time. They help learning professionals move through each phase of ADDIE with greater clarity and consistency. From diagnosing problems more effectively to generating stronger first drafts and improving evaluation, the value of prompting is not the output itself. It’s the quality of thinking it helps unlock.

The C.R.A.F.T. Framework · 04

The C.R.A.F.T. Framework.

Because great prompting is a craft, and every craft has a structure.

C.R.A.F.T. is a five-element prompt framework designed for learning and development professionals.

The name is intentional. Great prompting is a craft, and like any craft, it improves with the right structure.

Each element gives the AI the context, clarity and direction it needs to produce targeted, relevant outputs, so you spend less time re-prompting and more time designing.

Use it every time you write a prompt.

C
Context
Set the learning situation, audience and organisational context
R
Role
Define the AI's expertise and expert persona
A
Action
State the specific task you need completed
F
Format
Specify the structure, length and shape of the output
T
Tone
Match the voice, register and style to your audience
The Proof
Why context changes everything, same task, two very different results
❌ Weak Prompt

Create training for managers about change.

  • Generic definition of “change”
  • Generic communication tips
  • Generic action plan
Sounds like every course ever created
✓ C.R.A.F.T. Prompt

A hospital is implementing a new rostering system and frontline managers are resistant to the change. Act as a senior instructional designer and create a 30-minute workshop for this audience. Include practical coaching conversations and realistic scenarios. Present as a workshop outline. Use plain English.

  • ✔ Audience, context and business problem defined
  • ✔ Constraints and format specified
  • ✔ Expected outcome is clear
Targeted. Realistic. Ready to use.

Organised by what you need to do, not just by phase

AI-Enabled Prompts · Analyse · 06

AI-Enabled Prompts by Phase.

Clarify needs, gaps, audiences and business context.

01Analyse
Clarify needs, gaps, audiences, and business context.
PROMPT 1, Learning Needs Diagnosis

Stakeholder notes have been submitted outlining a proposed training request. Act as a learning consultant and identify the likely performance issue, capability gap, and whether training is the right solution. Present the output as a short diagnostic summary with recommendations. Use a clear, professional tone.

💬 C.R.A.F.T. Clarifier, append to this prompt

“Before creating the output, ask me any questions you need about the audience, business need, constraints, goals, or success measures.”

PROMPT 2, Learner Survey Analysis

Learner survey data has been collected from staff impacted by a new system rollout. Act as a senior instructional designer and identify patterns in confidence, knowledge gaps, barriers to performance, and priority development needs. Present the findings in a table with themes, evidence, and recommendations. Use plain English and concise business language.

💡 Before running this prompt

Paste your learner survey data directly above it.

PROMPT 3, Stakeholder Training Request Analysis

A stakeholder training request has been submitted for review. Act as a performance consultant and analyse the request to separate the stated symptoms from likely root causes. Identify the key diagnostic questions that should be asked before designing a solution. Flag any risks, assumptions, or gaps in the information provided. Indicate whether training is the most appropriate solution, a partial solution, or whether a non-training intervention is more likely to address the root cause. Present your findings in a structured table with these columns: Stated Request | Likely Symptoms | Possible Root Causes | Diagnostic Questions | Risks and Assumptions | Recommended Next Step. Use a clear, professional consulting tone.

© 2026 Trainerfy360, C.R.A.F.T. is a trademark of Trainerfy360trainerfy360.com
AI-Enabled Prompts · Design & Develop · 07

AI-Enabled Prompts by Phase.

Continued, Design & Develop

02Design
Structure learning, define outcomes, and shape the solution.
PROMPT 1, Learning Objectives Design

A workshop on leading teams through change is being designed for people leaders in a corporate organisation. Act as an instructional designer and create measurable learning objectives for the workshop. Align them to Bloom's Taxonomy and present them as a numbered list with one objective per line. Use plain English and a professional tone.

PROMPT 2, Learning Pathway Design

Frontline managers in a corporate organisation need to develop their ability to communicate change effectively. Act as a learning architect and design a blended learning pathway for this audience. Include recommended modalities, sequencing, and key learning outcomes. Present the output as a structured table. Use a clear, practical workplace tone.

💬 C.R.A.F.T. Clarifier, append to this prompt

“Before creating the output, ask me any questions you need about the audience, business need, constraints, goals, or success measures.”

03Develop
Turn design intent into real learning assets.
PROMPT 1, Training Content Development

A 6-slide PowerPoint training deck is being developed for people leaders supporting staff through organisational change. Act as a senior instructional designer and create the full content. Write the actual on-slide text for each slide, title, key messages, one framework slide, one scenario, one action-planning slide, and a summary. Include speaker notes and a suggested visual direction for each. Present as a slide-by-slide table. Use plain English and a professional workplace tone.

PROMPT 2, eLearning Storyboard

A 10-minute eLearning module on psychological safety is being developed for employees in a corporate workplace. Act as a digital learning designer and write a complete storyboard for the module. Include screen title, learning objective, on-screen content, narration, interaction type, and suggested visual treatment for each screen. Present the output in a structured table using clear, professional instructional design language.

© 2026 Trainerfy360, C.R.A.F.T. is a trademark of Trainerfy360trainerfy360.com
AI-Enabled Prompts · Implement & Evaluate · 08

AI-Enabled Prompts by Phase.

Continued, Implement & Evaluate

04Implement
Support launch, adoption, and learner readiness.
PROMPT 1, Manager Communication Pack

A new learning program is being rolled out across a corporate workforce and frontline and middle managers need structured communication support. Act as a change and learning consultant and create a manager communication pack. Include key messages, common questions, and practical actions managers should take before, during, and after launch. Present as a briefing document with clear headings. Use a supportive, practical tone.

PROMPT 2, Learner Launch Communication

A new compliance module is launching across a corporate organisation and all staff need to be informed and motivated to complete it. Act as a workplace learning advisor and create a learner-facing launch email. Explain why the training matters, what learners should expect, and what action is required. Present as a ready-to-send email. Use a clear, motivating, professional tone.

05Evaluate
Measure quality, impact, and opportunities to improve.
PROMPT 1, Post-Program Evaluation Survey

A manager development program has recently been completed and participant feedback needs to be gathered. Act as a learning evaluation specialist and create a post-program survey. Assess confidence, relevance, satisfaction, and application to role. Include a Net Promoter Score question. Present as a survey with rating-scale and open-ended questions. After presenting the survey questions, note how each section can be used to identify trends or inform future program decisions. Use a clear, neutral evaluation tone.

PROMPT 2, Learning Solution Review

A draft learning solution has been developed to support leaders through organisational change and requires a quality review. Act as a senior L&D consultant and evaluate its clarity, relevance, instructional quality, and alignment to business need. Present as strengths, risks, and recommendations. After presenting your recommendations, highlight which findings should most directly inform the next iteration of this solution. Use a constructive, professional tone.

💡 Before running this prompt

Paste your draft learning solution directly above it.

Practical Prompting Tips · 09

Practical Prompting Tips.

Simple ways to get stronger, more useful outputs

Good prompting is less about writing clever instructions and more about providing useful context. The quality of the output is often determined by the quality of the information, constraints and direction you provide. These simple practices help turn generic responses into outputs that are more relevant, practical and usable.

Use follow-up prompts

Your first prompt doesn't need to do everything. Use follow-up prompts to refine the output, tighten language, change tone, improve structure, or add missing detail.

Start fresh when the thread gets messy

If the output starts drifting, repeating, or mixing contexts, begin a new chat. A clean thread is often faster than trying to rescue a confused one.

Give one clear task at a time

Prompts work best when the request is specific. Avoid cramming too many unrelated tasks into one prompt unless you clearly structure them.

Refine, don't always restart

You don't need a brand-new prompt every time. Often the fastest path is to improve what you already have.

Upload reference material*

Upload relevant reference material first, brand guidelines, sample assets, style guides, policy documents, and existing learning materials.

Load brand guidelines & asset examples*

Brand rules explain what "good" looks like. Asset examples show how that looks in practice. Using both gives the model a much stronger reference point.

Tell it what to avoid

One of the easiest ways to improve output quality.
Example: Avoid jargon, overly academic language, and long paragraphs.

Ask for options

When tone, structure, or direction matters, ask for alternatives.
Example: Give me three versions: one concise, one more polished, one more conversational.

Ask it to flag assumptions

If your context is incomplete, the AI will fill gaps silently. Ask it to state any assumptions it has made before responding, this surfaces gaps before they become errors.

The C.R.A.F.T. Clarifier

Add this to any prompt to improve relevance before the AI writes anything:
"Before creating the output, ask me any questions you need about the audience, business need, constraints, goals, or success measures."
You’ll see this highlighted at the bottom of selected prompts throughout this guide.

🔒 Responsible AI UseOnly use content you are authorised to share. Avoid uploading confidential, sensitive, personal, or commercially restricted information unless it is approved and safe to do so.

Why This Exists
Helping Learning Professionals
Use AI With Confidence.
Practice-led approaches to AI-enabled learning design.
Amanda Jones
Instructional Designer
Founder, Trainerfy360

I’ve spent more than 20 years designing learning across corporate, government and higher education.

When AI arrived, I saw talented instructional designers facing a new challenge, not learning the technology, but figuring out how to use it without sacrificing quality, judgement and good learning design.

That’s why I created the C.R.A.F.T. Framework. Not because learning professionals need more prompts, but because they need a repeatable way to get better results from AI. Technology will continue to change. The ability to provide context, apply judgement and design effective learning experiences remains the real differentiator.

20+
Years in Learning Design
2
Industry Awards
